title: Lock Out And Tag Out The Operator Station From The Visu_Dual_MCP Screen
candidate_goal: Stop the system from the operator station HMI, optionally reposition the carriages if needed, then turn the operator station power switch OFF and apply lockout/tagout.
summary: The source provides a short operator station lockout/tagout sequence that starts on the Visu_Dual_MCP HMI screen, uses CYCLE STOP, optionally uses JOG with axis selection and + / - controls to reposition carriages, and ends by turning the operator station electrical panel power switch to OFF and applying LOTO.
rough_steps: At the operator station HMI, navigate to the "Visu_Dual_MCP" screen. [Image support needed: Operator station HMI screen for manually jogging the tipper axes during maintenance or positioning tasks.]
Press CYCLE STOP.
If carriage repositioning is needed before lockout/tagout, press JOG.
Select the corresponding axis for the tipper and use the + and - buttons to move the carriages. The source notes to see "Jogging the Tipper" on page 67 for details.

At the operator station electrical panel, turn the power switch to "OFF" and apply lockout/tagout. [Image support needed: Operator station HMI screen for manually jogging the tipper axes during maintenance or positioning tasks.]
expected_result: The system is stopped from the operator station, any needed carriage repositioning is completed using the documented jog controls, and the operator station power switch is turned OFF and locked/tagged out.
failure_or_escalation_notes: This candidate includes lockout/tagout and powered equipment positioning, so it should not be treated as a basic operator procedure without site authorization.
The source excerpt does not provide the detailed jogging instructions and refers to page 67 for those details.
Stop and escalate if safe lockout/tagout execution requires details not present in this excerpt.
access_or_tools_needed: Access to the operator station HMI
Access to the operator station electrical panel

source_refs.quote_or_summary: 1.2: OPERATOR STATION LOCKOUT/TAGOUT: At the operator station HMI, navigate to the "Visu_Dual_MCP" screen. Press CYCLE STOP. If you need to move the carriages to a different position, press JOG, select the corresponding axis for the tipper and use the + and - buttons to move the carriages. At the operator station electrical panel, turn the power switch to "OFF" and LOTO.
If the operator approaches or extends their hand into the lifting connecting rod assembly while the robot is lifting, it may cause serious personal injury.
Be aware of the movement trajectory of this product, and make sure to not interfere with the line.
Do not touch the tippers while the system is powered ON.

extraction_notes: Selected L2_support because the procedure explicitly includes LOTO and powered carriage movement, which is higher risk than normal operator activity.
Marked support_safe false because the excerpt contains hazardous energy and motion warnings.
Classified as recovery because the procedure is used to place equipment into a safe stopped and locked-out state rather than normal production operation.

evidence_source_refs.quote_or_summary: 1.2: OPERATOR STATION LOCKOUT/TAGOUT: At the operator station HMI, navigate to the "Visu_Dual_MCP" screen. Press CYCLE STOP. If you need to move the carriages to a different position, press JOG, select the corresponding axis for the tipper and use the + and - buttons to move the carriages. At the operator station electrical panel, turn the power switch to "OFF" and LOTO.
If the operator approaches or extends their hand into the lifting connecting rod assembly while the robot is lifting, it may cause serious personal injury.
Be aware of the movement trajectory of this product, and make sure to not interfere with the line.
Do not touch the tippers while the system is powered ON.

title: Verify The Normal Tote Exchange Sequence Coordinated By WCS
candidate_goal: Provide a source-backed diagnostic procedure candidate for checking whether the documented tote exchange sequence is occurring during normal operation.
summary: The source describes the expected sequence when a tote reaches a configured limit: the WCS stops diverting packages, an AGV retrieves the full tote, another AGV places an empty tote, and the WCS resumes sortation.
rough_steps: Observe that parcels are being directed into a tote during sorter operation.
Check whether the tote has reached the customer-specified weight, number of parcels, or volume threshold, if that state is available from site indicators or operations context.
Verify that the WCS sends a message to the sorter PLC to stop diverting packages when the tote limit is reached.
Verify that an AGV is triggered via the WCS controller to retrieve the full tote.
Verify that another AGV places an empty tote where the full tote was removed.
Verify that the WCS sends a message to the sorter PLC to resume sortation after the empty tote is in place.
expected_result: The observed tote exchange follows the documented sequence of stop diverting, retrieve the full tote, replace it with an empty tote, and resume sortation.
failure_or_escalation_notes: Escalate if the observed sequence does not match the documented order.
Escalate if the tote is not retrieved or replaced as described.
The source does not provide screen names, message text, or status fields, so verification may require site-specific tooling not defined here.
access_or_tools_needed: Ability to observe sorter and tote exchange activity
Access to WCS or sorter status indications if available at the site

source_refs.quote_or_summary: When the tote reaches the customer-specified weight, number of parcels, or volume, the WCS sends a message to the sorter PLC to stop diverting packages. An AGV is triggered via the WCS controller to retrieve the tote. Another AGV places an empty tote where the full tote was removed. The WCS then sends a message to the sorter PLC to resume sortation.

extraction_notes: This is a diagnostic candidate rather than an operation because the source mainly supports verifying the expected sequence, not performing direct user actions.
L1_support was chosen instead of operator because the procedure involves checking system coordination and controller-driven sequence behavior without source-backed operator controls.

evidence_source_refs.quote_or_summary: When the tote reaches the customer-specified weight, number of parcels, or volume, the WCS sends a message to the sorter PLC to stop diverting packages. An AGV is triggered via the WCS controller to retrieve the tote. Another AGV places an empty tote where the full tote was removed. The WCS then sends a message to the sorter PLC to resume sortation.

title: Identify Main Components At The Operator Station
candidate_goal: Help a user locate and identify the documented operator station components using the section text, Figure 3-1, and Table 3-1.
summary: This candidate converts the operator station overview and feature list into a procedural identification aid so a user can recognize the main station components in the documented area where parcels are received from the tippers.
rough_steps: Go to the operator station, the area where an operator receives parcels from the tippers.
Identify the two tippers at the operator station using the documented feature list. [Image support needed: Figure 3-1 showing the tipper locations at the operator station.]
Locate the chute at the operator station. [Image support needed: Figure 3-1 showing the chute area.]
Locate the two stacklights mounted on the top of the frame on either side of the chute. [Image support needed: Figure 3-1 showing stacklights on either side of the chute.]
Identify the plastic curtains at the operator station. [Image support needed: Figure 3-1 showing the plastic curtain position.]
Locate the operator station HMI. [Image support needed: Figure 3-1 showing the operator station HMI.]
Locate the push-buttons at the operator station; the section states there are four push-buttons. [Image support needed: Figure 3-1 showing the push-button area.]
Identify the rotary station at the operator station. [Image support needed: Figure 3-1 showing the rotary station.]
Compare the observed components to Table 3-1 to verify that the station features match the documented list.
expected_result: The user can identify the documented operator station components: tipper, chute, stacklight, plastic curtain, operator station HMI, push-buttons, and rotary station.
failure_or_escalation_notes: Escalate if the physical station does not match the documented feature list or figure.
Do not infer component names or locations beyond what is stated in the section text, Figure 3-1 metadata, and Table 3-1.

title: Determine Tipper Or Station Status From Operator Station Stacklights
candidate_goal: Use the documented operator station stacklight color, flashing pattern, and horn pattern to determine the current status of the corresponding tipper and station.
summary: This candidate converts the documented operator station stacklight meanings into a procedural check an operator can use to identify the current tipper or station status.
rough_steps: Locate the operator station stacklights at the top of either side of the frame.
Observe whether the displayed indication is solid or flashing.

Identify the displayed color or color combination and note whether a horn alarm is present and whether it is long or intermittent. [Image support needed: Operator Station HMI screen for the VISU_ALARM view showing tipper alarms and their timestamps.]

Compare the observed indication to the documented mappings: Solid Blue = tipper is paused; Solid Green = tipping in process; Solid Red = Estop is activated; Solid White = AGV is present (post-tipping); Solid Red, Yellow, Green = RMS error; Flashing Blue = tipper is disabled; Flashing Green = tipper is "starved"; Flashing Red = tipper is faulted; Flashing White = SurePost tote is being sent to the station; Flashing Yellow = AGV is present (pre-tipping); Flashing Green, Yellow, Green with long horn alarm = sort is starting; Flashing White with intermittent horn alarm = bagout is complete. [Image support needed: Operator Station HMI screen for the VISU_ALARM view showing tipper alarms and their timestamps.]
Record or communicate the documented status meaning for the corresponding tipper or station.
expected_result: The user can determine the documented current status of the corresponding tipper or station from the observed stacklight and horn pattern.
failure_or_escalation_notes: Escalate if the observed stacklight or horn pattern does not match any documented indication.
Do not infer corrective actions from the status mapping when the source provides status meaning only.
access_or_tools_needed: Visual access to the operator station stacklights
Ability to observe horn alarm presence and pattern

source_refs.quote_or_summary: Operator stations have two stacklights located at the top of either side of the frame. The stacklights provide information about the current status of the corresponding tipper and station, with listed solid and flashing color meanings and horn patterns.

title: Handle A Tote At The Hospital Station And Return An Empty Tote To The AGV

[Image support needed: Hospital HMI screen used when a tote bar code cannot be read at the Operator Station and must be handled manually at the Hospital Station.]
Use the green scanner to determine the reason the tote was brought to the hospital station.
Manually handle the tote at the hospital station as described by the station purpose in the source.
Observe that the AGV brings the tote to the right side of the hospital station from the operator perspective.
After the AGV moves to the left side of the hospital station, place an empty tote on the AGV.
Verify that the AGV has a tote to return with so it can re-enter the sorter area.

failure_or_escalation_notes: The source does not provide detailed manual handling steps for mis-sort, bad tip, or tote overload conditions; review is needed before converting this into a full runbook.

source_refs.quote_or_summary: In the event of a mis-sort, bad tip, or tote overload, the AGV is directed by the WCS to take the tote to a hospital station to be manually handled by an operator.

The AGV brings the tote to the right side (based on the perspective of the operator) of the hospital station, then moves to the left side where an operator places an empty tote on the AGV. The AGV will re-enter the sorter area after it has a tote to return with.

title: Start A Left Or Right Tipper Cycle Using The Operator Station Push-Buttons
candidate_goal: Use the correct flashing operator station push-button to initiate the left or right tipper cycle for a tote at the operator station.
summary: This candidate covers how an operator identifies which tipper side is ready, interprets the flashing green or yellow button for package type, and presses the corresponding button to start the tipper cycle.
rough_steps: Go to the operator station and identify the left tipper controls on the top two buttons and the right tipper controls on the bottom two buttons. [Image support needed: figure showing the operator station push-button layout with left tipper buttons on top, right tipper buttons on bottom, and the emergency stop location.]

Observe which tipper button is flashing for the tote that has arrived. [Image support needed: Photo of the operator station push-buttons used to start left or right tipper cycles, including Surepost tote cycles and the emergency stop.]

If the flashing button is green, identify it as the standard tipper cycle button for that tipper. [Image support needed: Photo of the operator station push-buttons used to start left or right tipper cycles, including Surepost tote cycles and the emergency stop.]

If the flashing button is yellow, identify it as the Surepost tipper cycle button for that tipper and press it only after the appropriate bag is ready for the Surepost packages. [Image support needed: Photo of the operator station push-buttons used to start left or right tipper cycles, including Surepost tote cycles and the emergency stop.]

Press the corresponding flashing button for the left or right tipper to initiate the tipping cycle. [Image support needed: Photo of the operator station push-buttons used to start left or right tipper cycles, including Surepost tote cycles and the emergency stop.]

If both sides are flashing because both sides have AGVs waiting, press the button for the side you are starting and observe that the opposite side stops flashing until the opposite cycle is done. [Image support needed: Photo of the operator station push-buttons used to start left or right tipper cycles, including Surepost tote cycles and the emergency stop.]

Do not rely on a button press made before a tote is gripped; the source states that a tip button pressed before a tote is in the gripper is ignored, and the corresponding button flashes only once the tote is gripped. [Image support needed: Photo of the operator station push-buttons used to start left or right tipper cycles, including Surepost tote cycles and the emergency stop.]
expected_result: The selected left or right tipper cycle is initiated using the correct operator station push-button for the tote and package type.
failure_or_escalation_notes: Do not use the emergency stop push-button as part of normal tipper cycle initiation.
If a button was pressed before a tote was in the gripper, expect that command to be ignored.
Escalate if the observed button behavior does not match the documented flashing behavior or if the cycle does not initiate after a valid flashing button press.
access_or_tools_needed: Physical access to the operator station
Visual access to the operator station push-buttons
Operator station push-button layout from Figure 3-3 and Table 3-2

source_refs.quote_or_summary: The operator station has four push-buttons and an emergency stop push-button. The top two buttons control the left tipper; the bottom two buttons control the right tipper.

When a tote comes into the tipper, the yellow button will start flashing to indicate that a tote with Surepost packages is being delivered to the tipper; the green button for that tipper will start flashing for all other packages.

The operator presses the corresponding green button to initiate the tipping cycle or presses the yellow button to initiate the tipping cycle after the appropriate bag is ready for the Surepost packages.

If a tipping cycle is in process for any tipper, and the other tipper has a tote, the other button will not start flashing until the cycle for the first tipper is completed. Both buttons can flash if both sides have AGVs and are waiting, but as soon as one tip button is pressed, the opposite button will stop flashing until the opposite cycle is done.
NOTE: If the tip button is pressed before a tote is in the gripper, the tipper will ignore that command. Only once a tote is gripped by the gripper, the corresponding tip button will flash.

extraction_notes: This is a normal operator action candidate grounded in the documented button meanings and flashing behavior.
The source does not provide detailed cycle completion confirmation indicators beyond button flashing behavior, so expected result is kept general.

If a tipping cycle is in process for any tipper, and the other tipper has a tote, the other button will not start flashing until the cycle for the first tipper is completed. Both buttons can flash if both sides have AGVs and are waiting, but as soon as one tip button is pressed, the opposite button will stop flashing until the opposite cycle is done.
NOTE: If the tip button is pressed before a tote is in the gripper, the tipper will ignore that command. Only once a tote is gripped by the gripper, the corresponding tip button will flash.

title: Use VISU_MANCONTROL Screen Manual Controls For Tipper Functions
candidate_goal: Use the VISU_MANCONTROL screen to perform documented manual control actions for the corresponding tipper, including gripper control, lamp test, fault reset, and tipper disable.
summary: This candidate captures the documented manual control actions available on the VISU_MANCONTROL screen shown for personnel logged in as maintenance. The source states the effects of GRIPPERS OPEN/CLOSE, LAMP TEST, LEFT/RIGHT TIPPER FAULT RESET, and LEFT/RIGHT TIPPER DISABLE.
rough_steps: Log in with maintenance-level access and open the VISU_MANCONTROL screen. [Image support needed: Figure 4-23 showing the Operator Station HMI Manual Control Screen seen by personnel logged in as maintenance.]
Identify the corresponding manual control for the intended tipper action: GRIPPERS OPEN/CLOSE, LAMP TEST, LEFT/RIGHT TIPPER FAULT RESET, or LEFT/RIGHT TIPPER DISABLE.

If using GRIPPERS OPEN/CLOSE, press the button for the corresponding tipper to manually open or close the gripper. [Image support needed: Manual control HMI screen for maintenance users showing tipper controls, status access, and alarm messaging on the operator station.]

If using LAMP TEST, press the LAMP TEST button and verify that the audible alarm sounds and the stacklight flashes red. [Image support needed: Manual control HMI screen for maintenance users showing tipper controls, status access, and alarm messaging on the operator station.]

If using LEFT/RIGHT TIPPER FAULT RESET, press the fault reset button for the corresponding tipper to reset it after a fault. [Image support needed: Manual control HMI screen for maintenance users showing tipper controls, status access, and alarm messaging on the operator station.]

If using LEFT/RIGHT TIPPER DISABLE, press the disable button for the corresponding tipper and verify that the stacklight illuminates solid blue. [Image support needed: Manual control HMI screen for maintenance users showing tipper controls, status access, and alarm messaging on the operator station.]

Do not use AIR BLOWOFF because the source states that this button is not currently used. [Image support needed: Manual control HMI screen for maintenance users showing tipper controls, status access, and alarm messaging on the operator station.]
expected_result: The selected manual control produces the documented effect on the corresponding tipper or station indication, such as gripper open/close action, audible alarm with flashing red stacklight during lamp test, tipper reset after a fault, or solid blue stacklight when the tipper is disabled.
failure_or_escalation_notes: Escalate if maintenance-level access is not available.
Escalate if the documented effect does not occur after pressing the selected control.
The source does not provide safety prerequisites, interlocks, or recovery detail for these manual controls; review is needed before support use.
access_or_tools_needed: Visual and audible access to the corresponding stacklight and alarm

title: Interpret Tipper Stats On The VISU_DATA Screen
candidate_goal: Use the Tipper Stats section on the VISU_DATA screen to identify left and right tipper counts, fault totals, average cycle time, average totes per hour, and time starved values.
summary: This candidate turns the documented Tipper Stats field meanings into a procedural check for reading and interpreting tipper performance and count metrics.
rough_steps: Open the VISU_DATA screen and locate the Tipper Stats section. [Image support needed: VISU_DATA screen showing the Tipper Stats section and its labeled fields.]
Read the Totes Processed (Left) fields and identify how many totes have been processed and how many have been sent to the Hospital Station for the left tipper.
Read the Totes Processed (Right) fields and identify how many totes have been processed and how many have been sent to the Hospital Station for the right tipper.
Read the Faults (Left/Right) fields and identify how many faults have occurred on each tipper since the last reset.
Read the Avg Cycle Time value and interpret it as the average time from when the gripper takes hold of the tote until it releases it back to the AGV.
Read the Avg Totes/Hour value and interpret it as the average number of totes processed at 15-minute intervals.

title: Start the Operator Station After the System Has Been Turned Off
candidate_goal: Bring the operator station and tipper axes from powered-off state to AUTO mode and ready to wait for WCS instructions.
summary: This candidate captures the documented startup sequence for the operator station, including power verification, ESTOP reset handling, homing the axes, cycle start, and placing the tipper in AUTO mode.
rough_steps: Verify the AGVs are powered on. The source references the P40A (V2.0) user manual, Document version V1.1 (20231206), for AGV startup instructions.
Verify the main disconnect is ON.

At the operator station control panel, check whether the ESTOP is flashing. [Image support needed: System screen artifact from the stopping and startup procedures for the OptiSweep system and operator station.]

If the ESTOP is flashing, pull out the ESTOP and press RESET on the HMI screen to clear any faults. [Image support needed: System screen artifact from the stopping and startup procedures for the OptiSweep system and operator station.]
Home every axis: Z1 and A1 for the left tipper, and Z2 and A2 for the right tipper.
Press AUTO REF.

Observe that the axis button changes from green to yellow to indicate it is moving. [Image support needed: System screen artifact from the stopping and startup procedures for the OptiSweep system and operator station.]
Observe that the tipper motor moves until it senses the home proximity switch, then stops.
Press CYCLE START.
Observe that the motor moves from home to ready.
Place the tipper in AUTO mode.
Verify the tipper waits for the WCS to send instructions.
expected_result: The axes are homed, the motor moves from home to ready, and the tipper is in AUTO mode waiting for WCS instructions.
failure_or_escalation_notes: The source does not provide escalation guidance if AGVs are not powered on, the main disconnect is not ON, faults do not clear, or axes do not home as expected.
The AGV startup details are not included in this source section and are only referenced to another manual.
access_or_tools_needed: Access to the operator station control panel
RESET control on the HMI screen
Ability to place the tipper in AUTO mode
Referenced P40A (V2.0) user manual for AGV startup instructions
extraction_notes: This startup procedure appears under the adjacent 5.3 content included in the provided section text, even though the section_id is for 5.2.
The source provides specific observed indicators: ESTOP flashing, axis button changing from green to yellow, motor moving to the home proximity switch, and motor moving from home to ready.
WCS is mentioned by the source, but no additional routing or dependency logic was added.

title: Recover a Non-Recoverable Operator Station Motor Fault by Manually Resetting and Repositioning the Tote
candidate_goal: Restore the operator station from a non-recoverable motor fault by using the MCP_Dual screen, clearing axis faults, repositioning the tote if needed, and returning the station to automatic operation.
summary: The source provides a manual recovery sequence for operator station motor faults that cannot be recovered in software, including a case where a tote is clamped by the gripper and may be lifted off the robot.
rough_steps: On the operator station HMI, navigate to the "MCP_Dual" screen using F3. [Image support needed: operator station HMI screen showing the MCP_Dual screen reference, axis labels, and related controls.]
Press CYCLE STOP to stop the tipping cycle. [Image support needed: operator station HMI area showing the CYCLE STOP control.]
Press JOG to enter manual jog mode, and verify the screen shows Manual Mode. [Image support needed: HMI view showing the JOG control and Manual Mode indication.]
Check whether any of the displayed axes show a fault, using the Z1 Axis, Z2 Axis, A1 Axis, and A2 Axis indicators.
If any axis shows a fault, press RESET to clear the fault. [Image support needed: HMI view showing a Fault indication and the RESET control associated with the axis display.]
If the tote is lifted off the robot, jog the Z-axis or A-axis, or both, to position the tote on or nearly on the robot using the plus and minus jog controls.
Use A1 and Z1 for the left axis, and use A2 and Z2 for the right tipper.
Press AUTO to enter automatic mode. [Image support needed: HMI view showing the AUTO control or automatic mode area.]
Press CYCLE START.
Observe that the grippers release and the robot is commanded to go to the hospital station.
After the robot has left the tipper, press RESET.
expected_result: The fault is manually reset, the tote is positioned on or nearly on the robot if needed, the grippers release, the robot leaves the tipper for the hospital station, and the station is returned from the documented non-recoverable fault condition.
failure_or_escalation_notes: Do not invent additional recovery actions beyond the documented sequence.
The source does not provide further steps if the fault does not clear, the tote cannot be positioned on or nearly on the robot, or the robot does not leave the tipper.
access_or_tools_needed: Access to the operator station HMI
Visibility of Z1 Axis, Z2 Axis, A1 Axis, and A2 Axis indicators

source_refs.quote_or_summary: If a fault occurs that is not able to be recovered via software, such as a motor overload fault, the station needs to be manually reset.
On the operator station HMI, navigate to the "MCP_Dual" screen (F3). Press CYCLE STOP to stop the tipping cycle.
Press JOG to enter manual jog mode. If any of the axes show a fault, press RESET to clear the fault.

If the tote is lifted off the robot, jog either the Z-axis or A-axis, or both, to position the tote on or nearly on the robot. NOTE: Use A1 and Z1 for the left axis; use A2 and Z2 for the right tipper.
Press AUTO to enter automatic mode. Press CYCLE START. The grippers will release and the robot will be commanded to go to the hospital station. After the robot has left the tipper, press RESET.

extraction_notes: This is a complete source-backed recovery procedure rather than a workflow.
Role was kept at operator because the source explicitly places the actions on the operator station HMI and does not mention guarded-area access, LOTO, or component replacement.
Inline image notes were added only for controls and screen elements supported by the source text and artifact metadata.

summary: This candidate isolates the documented HMI state check used in AGV fault recovery material to verify AGV readiness by confirming the displayed AGV state is "szonestaging."
rough_steps: Open or view the HMI area where AGV state is displayed. [Image support needed: HMI view or figure showing where AGV state appears.]
Identify the AGV state value for the AGVs being checked.
Verify that the displayed AGV state is "szonestaging".
Record or confirm that the AGVs are in the documented ready state before proceeding with restart or resume actions.
expected_result: The user confirms from the HMI whether the AGVs are displayed in the documented "szonestaging" state.
failure_or_escalation_notes: Stop and escalate if the HMI does not show AGV state or if the AGVs are not shown as "szonestaging" when this verification is required.
Do not infer equivalent AGV states that are not explicitly documented in the source.
access_or_tools_needed: Access to the HMI showing AGV state

title: Add an AGV to a Zone When No Empty AGV Is Available
candidate_goal: Restore operation by adding an AGV into a zone under the tote rack and confirming the AGV reaches the documented visual and HMI state.
summary: This candidate captures the documented fault recovery procedure for adding an AGV to a zone with no empty AGV, including stopping RMS, moving an AGV into the zone, adding it in RMS, checking the blue indication, verifying the HMI state "szonestaging," and resuming RMS.
rough_steps: E-stop RMS.

Move an AGV into the zone under the tote rack. [Image support needed: Figure 5-3 or source-backed screen/diagram showing the Add AGV to Zone with No Empty AGV screen or the relevant zone context if available.]
Add the AGV in RMS using the Tote API Controls referenced by the manual.
Wait for the AGV to turn blue.

Verify on the HMI that the AGV status/state is "szonestaging." [Image support needed: Screen associated with the fault recovery procedure for adding an AGV to a zone when there is no empty AGV available.]
If the AGV is moving to the Hospital, remove the AGV and add it again.
Press the E-stop again.
Resume RMS using the documented system start procedure.
expected_result: The AGV is added to the zone, turns blue, shows status/state "szonestaging" on the HMI, and RMS can be resumed.
failure_or_escalation_notes: If the AGV does not show the documented "szonestaging" state, do not assume another acceptable state.
If the AGV moves to the Hospital, the source instructs the user to remove the AGV and add it again.
This procedure includes E-stop use and physically moving an AGV into the zone under the tote rack, so a higher safer role is selected pending review.
access_or_tools_needed: Ability to view AGV color/status indication
Physical access to move an AGV into the zone under the tote rack

title: Recover Empty AGVs That Lost Tasks After an RMS Restart
candidate_goal: Restore empty AGVs after task loss by removing and re-adding them, confirming the documented HMI state, and resuming RMS.
summary: This candidate captures the documented fault recovery procedure for empty AGVs that lost tasks after an RMS restart, including stopping RMS, removing and re-adding AGVs without totes twice, checking for the "szonestaging" state, and resuming RMS.
rough_steps: E-stop RMS.
Remove and re-add AGVs without totes twice using the Tote API Controls referenced by the manual.

If the system is to be restarted, verify on the HMI that the AGVs are in the "szonestaging" state. [Image support needed: Screen associated with the fault recovery procedure for adding an AGV to a zone when there is no empty AGV available.]
Press the E-stop again when ready to resume.
Resume RMS using the documented system start procedure.
expected_result: The empty AGVs are reintroduced through the documented remove/re-add process, are confirmed in the "szonestaging" state when applicable, and RMS is resumed.
failure_or_escalation_notes: If AGVs are not in the documented "szonestaging" state before restart-related actions, do not assume readiness.

title: Recover From AGV Fault During Exchange
candidate_goal: Restore the exchange location after a lifted AGV faults while leaving the RMS by stopping the RMS, removing affected AGV(s), and reintroducing a tote and empty AGV in the zone.
summary: This candidate captures the documented fault-recovery procedure for an AGV that faults during exchange while lifted and leaving the RMS, including checking whether the trailing AGV back fills and removing it if it does not.
rough_steps: E-stop the RMS.
Identify the lifted AGV that faulted while leaving the RMS during the exchange.
Remove the faulted AGV from the RMS using the documented AGV API Controls reference on page 24.
Check whether the AGV behind the faulted AGV back fills.
If the AGV behind it does not back fill, remove that AGV as well using the documented AGV API Controls reference on page 24.
Introduce a new tote at that location.
Place an empty AGV in that zone.
expected_result: The faulted lifted AGV is removed from the RMS, any non-back-filling trailing AGV is also removed, and the affected location is repopulated with a new tote and an empty AGV in the zone.
failure_or_escalation_notes: The source does not provide detailed instructions for how to introduce the new tote or place the empty AGV in the zone; review supporting source material before execution.
The source does not describe what to do if AGV removal through AGV API Controls cannot be completed.
Choose a higher-support role because the procedure involves fault recovery and system-level AGV removal from the RMS.
access_or_tools_needed: Access to the RMS E-stop control
Access to AGV API Controls referenced on page 24
Ability to identify the faulted lifted AGV and the trailing AGV

title: Recover an AGV That Lost a Tote Without Faulting
candidate_goal: Restore an AGV and tote to service after an AGV loses a tote without faulting by moving the AGV to the Hospital, cycling AGV power, re-registering the AGV, and removing and re-adding the tote.
summary: The source provides a recovery procedure for an AGV that lost a tote but did not fault. It includes E-stop, brake release use, manually pushing the AGV to the Hospital, removing and re-adding the AGV at the Hospital station PC, power cycling the AGV, checking for green AGV lights, positioning it over a QR code, and removing and re-adding the missing tote.
rough_steps: E-stop the RMS.
Press the brake release on the AGV that lost a tote.
Push the AGV over to the Hospital.
At the Hospital station PC, remove the AGV and then add the AGV back into the system.
Turn the AGV off, then turn it on again.
When the AGV lights turn green, roll it over the QR code in front of the Inbound Hospital Station.
Locate the missing tote and bring it to the Hospital to remove it from the system.
Add the tote back into the system. The AGV should then take the tote back to the sorter.
expected_result: The AGV is reintroduced into the system, the tote is removed and added back, the AGV lights turn green, the AGV is positioned over the QR code at the Inbound Hospital Station, and the AGV should take the tote back to the sorter.
failure_or_escalation_notes: Escalate if the brake release cannot be used or the AGV cannot be moved to the Hospital.
Escalate if the AGV cannot be removed and added back at the Hospital station PC.
Escalate if the AGV lights do not turn green after the power cycle.
Escalate if the missing tote cannot be located or cannot be removed and re-added using the documented tote procedures.
access_or_tools_needed: Access to the AGV brake release
Access to the Hospital station PC
Access to the Inbound Hospital Station QR code location
Access to documented Add/Remove AGV procedure
Access to documented Remove Tote procedure
Access to documented Add Tote procedure

Referenced source: 1.7 Add/Remove AGV, page 52
Referenced source: 1.2 Remove Tote, page 47
Referenced source: 1.1 Add Tote, page 46

The source explicitly provides one observable check: AGV lights turn green; this was preserved as the only stated status indicator.

Press the brake release on the AGV that lost a tote.

Add the tote back into the system using the documented Add Tote procedure reference on page 46.

title: Process a Tote at the Hospital Station
candidate_goal: Extract an arriving tote from the system at the hospital station, identify it by barcode scan, perform the task indicated by the HMI status fields, and return the tote to the system when needed.
summary: This candidate captures the normal hospital station tote-handling procedure described in the manual, including AGV delivery side, HMI screen use, barcode scanning with the green scanner, Tote ID field verification, task execution based on status fields, and return placement on the induct side.
rough_steps: Confirm the arriving tote has been brought by the AGV to the right side of the hospital station. [Image support needed: hospital station view showing the right side where the AGV delivers the tote.]

Open the corresponding screen on the HMI to extract the tote from the system, using the information shown in the status fields to determine the correct screen. [Image support needed: HMI screen area with status fields used to identify the corresponding screen.]
Scan the tote barcode using the green scanner. [Image support needed: green scanner at the hospital station used for tote barcode scanning.]
Verify that the tote ID automatically populates the Tote ID field after scanning. [Image support needed: HMI Tote ID field showing where the scanned tote ID appears.]
Perform the required task for the tote as determined by the information in the status fields.
If the tote is going back into the system, place it on the induct side on the left side of the station. [Image support needed: left-side induct area of the hospital station for tote return.]
Ensure the tote is flush against the end stops. [Image support needed: end stops on the induct side showing correct tote alignment.]
expected_result: The tote is extracted and identified at the hospital station, the required task is completed based on the HMI status fields, and any tote returning to the system is placed on the left-side induct area flush against the end stops.
failure_or_escalation_notes: If the Tote ID field does not automatically populate after scanning, the source does not provide recovery steps.
If the correct HMI screen or required task cannot be determined from the status fields, the source does not provide further guidance.
access_or_tools_needed: HMI access to the corresponding hospital station screen
Visibility of status fields on the HMI
Access to the Tote ID field on the HMI
Physical access to the right side and left-side induct area of the station

source_refs.quote_or_summary: When a tote arrives at a hospital station: An AGV brings a tote to the right side of the hospital station.
Open the corresponding screen on the HMI to extract the tote from the system (determined by information in the status fields).
Scan the tote barcode using the green scanner. The tote ID should automatically populate the Tote ID field after scanning.
Perform whatever task needs to be performed as determined by the information in the status fields.
If a tote is going back into the system, put the tote on the induct side (left side) of the station. Ensure the tote is flush against the end stops.
Artifact appears on page 92 in section optisweep_operation_and_maintenance_manual_final_1_5_9_hospital_station_operation.

title: Inspect And Lubricate Tipper Z-Axis Linear Bearings
candidate_goal: Check the tipper Z-axis linear rails for grease condition and lubricate the bearing blocks when the rails are dry.
summary: The source describes a preventive maintenance procedure for visually inspecting both Z-axis linear rails for wet grease or dried grease and lubricating the bearing blocks if the rails are dry.
rough_steps: Visually inspect both Z-axis linear rails for wetness of grease and for signs of dried grease. [Image support needed: source-backed view identifying both Z-axis linear rails and the grease condition to inspect.]
Determine whether the rails are dry, meaning not wet with grease.
If the rails are dry, lubricate the bearing blocks using automotive bearing grease such as Mobil1™.

Locate the two bearing blocks and grease the standard 1/4-in. nipple on each end of each block, for a total of four nipples. [Image support needed: source-backed view showing the bearing blocks and nipple locations.]
Add 1.0 to 3.0 cc of grease to each bearing block.
Do not treat wiping grease on the rails as adequate lubrication.
expected_result: The Z-axis linear rails have been checked for grease condition, and dry rails are addressed by lubricating each bearing block through the documented grease nipples with the specified grease quantity.
failure_or_escalation_notes: Use a higher-safe role because this is preventive maintenance involving component lubrication and source text does not define operator-safe access conditions.
Do not substitute wiping grease on the rails for bearing block lubrication.
access_or_tools_needed: Visual access to both Z-axis linear rails

Adding 1.0 to 3.0 cc of grease to each block should be adequate.
NOTE: Wiping grease on the rails is not adequate.

title: Install Replacement Motor Onto Gearbox
candidate_goal: Install a replacement motor onto the gearbox with the documented alignment, orientation, fasteners, and torque values.
summary: This candidate captures the documented motor installation procedure within the motor replacement section, including shaft cleaning, motor verification, clamp collar alignment, motor mounting, torque application, electrical reconnection, and restart preparation.
rough_steps: Clean the motor shaft of any grease or debris.
Verify the correct motor is being installed: A-axis left, A-axis right, Z-axis left, or Z-axis right.
Inside the gearbox, align the opening in the shaft bushing with the opening in the clamp collar.

Insert the hex key through the access hole into the clamp collar fastener to help prevent the clamp collar from rotating. [Image support needed: diagram showing the access hole and clamp collar location on the gearbox.]

Align the key-way or flat in the motor shaft, if present, with the opening in the clamp collar or bushing and fully insert the motor shaft. [Image support needed: diagram showing motor shaft key-way or flat alignment with the clamp collar or bushing opening.]
Temporarily tighten the clamp collar and do not over-tighten.

Mount the motor to the gearbox using four M5 socket-head screws and tighten them to 7 Nm (62 in-lb). [Image support needed: diagram showing the four M5 socket-head screw mounting points between the motor and gearbox.]
Orient the motor with the electrical connections facing down. [Image support needed: diagram showing the correct motor orientation and electrical connections facing down.]
Loosen then re-tighten the clamp collar using the axis-specific tool and torque: Z-axis uses a 4-mm hex wrench to 9.5 Nm (84 in-lb); A-axis uses a 5-mm hex wrench to 16.5 Nm (146 in-lb).
Replace the access hole plug and reconnect the electrical connections. [Image support needed: diagram showing the access hole plug plus communication and power cable connection points.]
If the motor has not been configured with the manufacturer application, follow the documented configuration steps.
Re-install the top and or bottom side guarding, if necessary.
Re-start the operator station using the documented startup procedure.
expected_result: The replacement motor is installed on the gearbox, secured with the specified fasteners and torque values, oriented with electrical connections facing down, reconnected, and the operator station is restarted.
failure_or_escalation_notes: Do not over-tighten the clamp collar during the temporary tightening step.
Escalate if the correct motor variant cannot be verified for A-axis left, A-axis right, Z-axis left, or Z-axis right.
Escalate if the motor cannot be fully inserted with the documented shaft and clamp collar or bushing alignment.
Escalate if motor configuration with the manufacturer application is required but not available in the provided source section.
access_or_tools_needed: 4-mm hex wrench for Z-axis clamp collar
5-mm hex wrench for A-axis clamp collar
Torque tool capable of 7 Nm, 9.5 Nm, and 16.5 Nm
Manufacturer application if motor configuration is required
Access to operator station startup procedure

title: Install a Replacement Gripper on the A-axis
candidate_goal: Install the correct left or right replacement gripper onto the A-axis gearbox face, secure it with the specified fasteners and torque, reconnect the cable carrier, electrical connectors, and air line, and prepare the assembly for later commissioning.
summary: This candidate captures the documented installation portion of gripper replacement, including LOTO, mechanical mounting, counter-balance attachment, cable routing, connector reconnection, air line connection, valve cover reinstallation, and operator station restart reference.
rough_steps: Verify the correct replacement gripper has been selected, specifically left gripper or right gripper, and manually position the Z-axis or A-axis to simplify alignment.
LOTO the gripper using the referenced Operator Station Lockout/Tagout procedure.
Apply thread locker (blue Loctite®) to all fasteners used in these installation steps.

Align the gripper bolt pattern to the A-axis gearbox face and ensure the gripper and gearbox orientation allows all 10 M6x20 socket-head screws to be inserted. [Image support needed: figure or page image showing gripper-to-gearbox alignment and bolt pattern orientation.]
Install the 10 M6x20 gripper fasteners; the source notes it may be easier to install two fasteners on opposite sides of the bolt circle first, then insert the remaining fasteners.
Tighten the 10 M6 gripper fasteners to 16.5 Nm (146 in-lb).
Note that the A-axis may drift down after the gripper weight is added; the source states this is acceptable.

Attach the counter-balance weight to the counter-balance carriage using four M10 socket head screws with blue Loctite® and tighten to 63 Nm (47 ft-lb). The source states the counter-balance weight is approximately 3-in. x 4-in. x 13-in., one or two pieces, and weighs a total of 43 lbs (19.4 kg). [Image support needed: figure or page image showing counter-balance location and attachment hardware.]
Attach the A-axis cable carrier to the gripper using two M3x8 flat head screws (included). [Image support needed: figure or page image showing cable carrier attachment point on the gripper.]

Feed the M8 connectors from the cable through the grommet one at a time, then push the end of the jacketed cable through the grommet. [Image support needed: figure or page image showing grommet and cable routing path.]
Feed the capped air line through the grommet. [Image support needed: figure or page image showing air line and M8 connections passing through the grommet.]
Remove the valve cover by removing the four M4x50 socket-head screws.
Connect the M8x3 connector labeled "Valve" to the valve. [Image support needed: figure or page image identifying the valve connection.]
Connect the M8x4 connector to the tote detect sensor. [Image support needed: figure or page image identifying the tote detect sensor connection.]
Connect the M8x3 connector labeled LONG to the longer Gripper Sensor.
Connect the M8x3 connector labeled SHORT to the shorter Gripper Sensor.
Remove the air line cap and valve fitting plug, if present.
Connect the supply air line by fully inserting the line into the valve fitting.

Install the valve cover using the four M4x50 socket head screws, taking care not to pinch any wires along the cover edges and inserting the grommet into the notch for the tote sensor wire. [Image support needed: figure or page image showing valve cover, wire routing, and grommet notch placement.]
Refer to the Tipper Commissioning procedure for leveling and commissioning the new gripper.
Re-start the operator station using the referenced Starting The Operator Station procedure.
expected_result: The replacement gripper is mechanically installed, the counter-balance is attached, cable carrier and connectors are reconnected, the air line is connected, the valve cover is reinstalled without pinched wires, and the system is ready for the separate leveling and commissioning procedure.
failure_or_escalation_notes: Do not proceed if the selected gripper is not confirmed as the correct left or right gripper.
Use LOTO as referenced by the source before performing installation work.
Stop if all 10 M6x20 screws cannot be inserted due to orientation or alignment issues.
Stop if wiring cannot be routed without pinching at the valve cover edges or if the grommet cannot be seated in the tote sensor wire notch.
This procedure references a separate Tipper Commissioning procedure for leveling and commissioning; installation alone does not complete that work.
access_or_tools_needed: Access to the gripper and A-axis assembly

title: Remove the A-Axis Gearbox
candidate_goal: Remove the A-axis gearbox from the carriage using the documented mechanical removal procedure.
summary: This candidate captures the documented A-axis gearbox removal procedure, including LOTO, side panel removal, gripper removal, counter-balance drift awareness, possible motor removal for access, and removal of the gearbox mounting screws.

title: Install the A-Axis Gearbox
candidate_goal: Mount the A-axis gearbox in the correct orientation, secure it with the specified fasteners and torque, reinstall referenced components, and return the operator station to service.
summary: This candidate covers the documented installation procedure for the A-axis gearbox, including orientation of the clamp collar access hole, fastener installation and torque, reinstallation of related components, panel reassembly, and operator station restart.
rough_steps: Lift the A-axis gearbox into place.
Orient the clamp collar access hole toward the rounded side of the carriage. [Image support needed: figure or page image showing the clamp collar access hole and the rounded side of the carriage.]
Install the eight (8) M5x18 socket-head screws.
Tighten the eight screws to 5.1 Nm (45.1 in-lb).
Use blue or red Loctite® on the fasteners.
Install the motor into the gearbox using the referenced "Motor Installation" procedure on page 108.
Install the gripper onto the system using the referenced "Installation" procedure on page 100.

Install the counter-balance weights if they were previously removed, using the referenced installation procedure on page 96. [Image support needed: figure or page image showing the counterbalances, counterbalance carriage, or their installed position if available.]
Reinstall the top and/or bottom side panels if necessary.
Re-start the operator station using the referenced "Starting The Operator Station" procedure on page 66.
expected_result: The A-axis gearbox is installed with the clamp collar access hole facing the rounded side of the carriage, secured with eight M5x18 socket-head screws tightened to 5.1 Nm (45.1 in-lb) with blue or red Loctite®, related components are reinstalled, panels are back in place as needed, and the operator station is restarted.
failure_or_escalation_notes: Escalate if the gearbox cannot be oriented with the clamp collar access hole toward the rounded side of the carriage.
Escalate if the specified hardware is unavailable or if the required torque or Loctite® application cannot be completed.
This section depends on other referenced procedures for motor, gripper, counter-balance, and operator station restart details.

title: Remove Z-Axis Gearbox Access Components and Release Belt Drive for Gearbox Replacement
candidate_goal: Perform the documented removal preparation steps for Z-axis gearbox replacement, including positioning, lockout/tagout, access panel removal, carriage securing, belt clamp removal, and belt tension release.
summary: This candidate captures the documented removal procedure for the Z-axis gearbox replacement section up to the optional motor removal reference. The steps involve manual positioning through the HMI, LOTO, panel removal, securing the carriages, disconnecting belt clamps, and releasing belt tension around the gearbox area.
rough_steps: Use the HMI to manually position (jog) the A-axis and counter-balance between the threaded holes in the actuator base. Verify there are two holes adjacent to each rail and that the carriages are positioned between the holes on the respective rails. [Image support needed: figure or diagram showing the actuator base threaded holes, rails, carriage positions, and gearbox area.]
LOTO the tipper using the referenced Operator Station Lockout/Tagout procedure.

Use a 3/8-in. wrench or socket to remove the nine self-tapping fasteners securing the side panel, then remove the station side panel to access the motor. [Image support needed: Maintenance figure for the gearbox referenced in the A-axis removal and re-assembly procedure.]

Insert an M6 socket-head screw into the holes below both carriages to prevent the A-axis and counter-balance from dropping once belt tension is released. [Image support needed: figure or diagram showing the holes below both carriages and the intended screw insertion points.]

Remove the A-axis belt clamp and the counter-balance belt clamp by removing the six M6 screws in each clamp. Verify both carriages are free from the belt after the clamps are removed. [Image support needed: source figure or procedure image showing the belt clamps and M6x20 screw locations.]

Release belt tension by loosening the two M6x35 socket-head tensioning screws on the lower end. [Image support needed: source figure or procedure image showing the lower-end M6 tensioning screw locations.]

Loosen the two M8 socket-head screws that lock the idler pulley assembly in place. [Image support needed: source figure or procedure image showing the idler pulley assembly and the two M8 socket-head screws.]
If needed, remove the Z-axis motor using the separately referenced Motor Removal procedure on page 106.
expected_result: The side panel is removed, the carriages are secured against dropping, the belt clamps are removed, belt tension is released, and the idler pulley assembly lock screws are loosened so the gearbox removal can proceed.
failure_or_escalation_notes: This procedure includes LOTO and mechanical work around belt tension and suspended axis components, so it is not operator-safe.
Stop if the A-axis and counter-balance cannot be positioned as described between the threaded holes in the actuator base.
Stop if the carriage securing screws cannot be installed below both carriages before belt tension is released.
Use the separately referenced Motor Removal procedure for motor removal details; do not invent missing steps from this section alone.
access_or_tools_needed: HMI access for manual jog positioning
M6 socket-head screws for carriage securing
Access to the station side panel, motor area, belt clamps, tensioning screws, and idler pulley assembly

title: Remove Timing Belt From Tipper Assembly
candidate_goal: Safely remove the timing belt from the pulleys during timing belt replacement.
summary: This candidate captures the documented removal portion of the timing belt replacement procedure, including positioning the A-axis and counterbalance, locking out the tipper, opening access panels, securing the carriages with M6 screws, removing belt clamps, releasing belt tension, and removing the belt from the pulleys when only the timing belt is being replaced.
rough_steps: Gather the documented replacement part, PPE, and tools: timing belt, safety glasses, gloves, torque wrench, 5-mm hex wrench, 6-mm hex wrench, 3/8-in. wrench or socket, Loctite, and sonic belt tension meter.

Manually position the A-axis and the counterbalance between the threaded holes in the actuator base. Verify there are two holes adjacent to each rail and that the carriages are positioned between the holes on the respective rails. [Image support needed: figure or diagram showing timing belt location, A-axis and counterbalance position relative to actuator base holes, and carriage position on the rails.]
LOTO the tipper using the referenced Operator Station Lockout/Tagout procedure.

Use a 3/8-in. wrench or socket to remove the nine self-tapping fasteners securing the side panels, then remove the station side panels to access the timing belt. [Image support needed: figure or diagram showing the access area behind the station side panels and timing belt location.]

Thread M6 socket-head screws into the holes below both carriages to prevent the A-axis and counterbalance from dropping once belt tension is released. [Image support needed: figure or diagram showing the threaded holes used for the M6 socket-head screws below both carriages.]

Remove the belt clamps by removing the six M6x20 countersunk screws from each clamp with a 5-mm hex wrench to free the carriages from the belt. [Image support needed: figure or diagram identifying the belt clamps and M6x20 screws.]

Release belt tension by loosening the two M6x35 socket-head screws on the bottom side of the assembly and loosening the two M8x25 socket-head screws holding the idler pulley assembly in place. [Image support needed: figure or diagram identifying the M6 tensioning screws and M8 socket-head screws on the idler pulley assembly.]

If only the timing belt is being replaced, slide the idler pulley assembly up and re-tighten the two M6x35 socket-head screws to hold the idler pulley assembly in place, then remove the timing belt from the pulleys.
expected_result: The timing belt is removed from the pulleys, with the A-axis and counterbalance secured against dropping during belt tension release.
failure_or_escalation_notes: This procedure includes LOTO and mechanical disassembly, so it is not support-safe for operator execution.
Stop if the A-axis, counterbalance, or carriages cannot be positioned as described before belt tension is released.
Stop if the securing screws or belt clamp fasteners cannot be identified or removed as documented.
Do not proceed beyond the documented removal scope when the source does not provide additional recovery or adjustment guidance in this section.

Ability to perform Operator Station Lockout/Tagout

title: Install the Z-Axis Gearbox and Set Belt Tension
candidate_goal: Reinstall the Z-axis gearbox, reconnect related drive components, tension the belt to the documented specification, and return the assembly to an installed state.
summary: This candidate captures the documented Z-axis gearbox installation procedure, including gearbox insertion, pulley and belt placement, motor orientation, idler adjustment, sonic tension measurement, clamp reattachment, panel reinstallation, and operator station restart references.
rough_steps: Insert the gearbox into the pilot hole. [Image support needed: figure or page image showing the gearbox entering the pilot hole.]
Mount a timing pulley on the gearbox using the referenced Installation procedure on page 168.
Loop the belt over the pulley.
Fully seat the gearbox into the pilot hole and verify the clamp collar access hole is oriented up. [Image support needed: figure or page image showing the clamp collar access hole orientation.]
Mount the gearbox with four M6x20 socket-head screws with Loctite® and tighten to 7 Nm (62 in-lb).
Mount the Z-axis motor using the referenced Motor Installation procedure on page 108 and verify the motor electrical connectors are oriented down.
Slightly lift the counter-balance carriage, remove the M6 fasteners supporting it, and lower the counter-balance carriage so it rests on the lower hard stop.
Very lightly tighten the M8x25 screws holding the idler pulley assembly so the assembly is not loose but can still slide under the fasteners.
Thread the two M6x35 tension screws until they make firm contact with the actuator base.
Continue to equally tighten the two tension screws, turning both screws the exact same amount so they apply the same amount of force.
Measure belt tension only on the counter-balance leg of the belt and gradually increase tension until 471 N is reached, with an acceptable range of 448 N to 494 N.
If using a Gates 508c Sonic Tension Meter, use Mass = 4.86 g/m, Width = 20 mm, and Span = 1200 mm.
Adjust and check the tension until three acceptable readings in a row are received, noting that excessive area noise can interfere with sonic meter readings.
Tighten the M8x25 fasteners to 21.2 Nm (188 in-lbs) to lock the idler assembly in place.
Raise the counter-balance carriage back to mid-travel and insert the M6 socket-head screws for the carriage to rest against.

Re-attach the A-axis belt clamp using six M6 screws and Loctite®, making sure the belt clamp aligns with the belt teeth and does not pinch the belt. Move the belt slightly if needed for alignment. [Image support needed: figure or page image showing belt clamp alignment with belt teeth.]

Re-attach the counterbalance belt clamp using six M6 screws and Loctite®, making sure the belt clamp aligns with the belt teeth and does not pinch the belt. Lift the counterbalance slightly if needed for alignment. [Image support needed: figure or page image showing counterbalance belt clamp alignment.]
Remove the M6 socket-head screws supporting the A-axis and counter-balance.
Reinstall the top and bottom side panels.
Re-start the operator station using the referenced Starting The Operator Station procedure on page 66.
expected_result: The Z-axis gearbox is installed, the gearbox and motor are oriented as documented, the belt is tensioned within the specified range with three acceptable readings in a row, clamps are reattached without pinching the belt, panels are reinstalled, and the operator station is restarted.
failure_or_escalation_notes: Stop if the gearbox cannot be fully seated in the pilot hole or the clamp collar access hole cannot be oriented up as documented.
Stop if the motor electrical connectors cannot be oriented down as documented.
Stop if equal adjustment of the two tension screws cannot be maintained.
Stop if belt tension cannot be brought into the documented 448 N to 494 N range or three acceptable readings in a row cannot be obtained.
Be aware that excessive area noise can interfere with sonic meter readings.
Stop if the belt clamp cannot align with the belt teeth or if the belt is being pinched.
access_or_tools_needed: Access to the Z-axis gearbox assembly
Six M6 screws for the A-axis belt clamp
Six M6 screws for the counterbalance belt clamp
Torque tool capable of 7 Nm and 21.2 Nm
Gates 508c Sonic Tension Meter if used
Access to the referenced Installation procedure on page 168
Access to the referenced Motor Installation procedure on page 108
Access to the referenced Starting The Operator Station procedure on page 66

title: Install Replacement Timing Belt and Set Belt Tension
candidate_goal: Install a new timing belt, set the belt tension to the documented range using the specified sonic tension meter settings, and secure the idler assembly and belt clamps.
summary: This candidate captures the documented installation portion of timing belt replacement, including pulley placement, counter-balance carriage positioning, equal tension screw adjustment, sonic tension verification, fastener torque, clamp reattachment, panel reinstallation, and operator station restart reference.
rough_steps: Place the new timing belt on the pulleys.
Slightly lift the counter-balance carriage, remove the M6 fastener supporting it, and lower the counter-balance carriage to rest on the lower hard stop.
Very lightly tighten the M8x25 screws holding the idler pulley assembly so the assembly is not loose but can still slide under the fasteners.
Insert the two M6x35 tension screws until they make firm contact with the actuator base.
Equally tighten the two tension screws to add belt tension, making sure both screws are turned the exact same amount so they apply the same amount of force.
Check belt tension only on the counter-balance leg of the belt.
If using a Gates 508c Sonic Tension Meter, set Mass to 4.86 g/m, Width to 20 mm, and Span to 1200 mm.

Gradually increase and adjust the belt tension until 471 N is reached, with an acceptable range between 448 N and 494 N, and continue adjusting/checking until three acceptable readings in a row are received.
Account for excessive area noise because forklifts beeping, conveyors running, or similar noise can interfere with sonic meter readings.
Tighten the M8x25 fasteners to 21.2 Nm (188 in-lbs) to lock the idler assembly in place.
Raise the counter-balance carriage back to mid-travel and insert the M6 socket-head screws for the carriage to rest against.

Re-attach the A-axis belt clamp using the six M6 screws and Loctite®, making sure the clamp aligns with the belt teeth and does not pinch the belt; move the belt slightly if needed so the clamp lines up properly.

Re-attach the counter-balance belt clamp using the six M6 screws and Loctite®, making sure the clamp aligns with the belt teeth and does not pinch the belt; lift the counter-balance slightly if needed so the clamp lines up properly.
Remove the M6 socket-head screws supporting the A-axis and counter-balance.
Reinstall the side panels.
Re-start the operator station using the referenced 'Starting The Operator Station' procedure on page 66.
expected_result: The replacement timing belt is installed, tension is verified within the documented acceptable range with three acceptable readings in a row, the idler assembly and belt clamps are secured, side panels are reinstalled, and the operator station is restarted.
failure_or_escalation_notes: Stop if equal adjustment of the two tension screws cannot be maintained, because the source states both screws need to apply the same amount of force.
Stop if acceptable belt tension readings cannot be obtained or if three acceptable readings in a row are not achieved.
Be aware that excessive ambient noise can interfere with sonic tension meter readings.
Escalate if the belt clamps cannot be aligned with the belt teeth without pinching the belt.
This procedure involves mechanical work on belt, carriage, pulley, and clamp components and is safer for L2_support.

title: Remove the ClearLink (CLINK) Controller

title: Install Tip or A-Axis Cable
candidate_goal: Install and route a new Tip or A-axis cable, connect its labeled M8 connectors to the documented components, secure the cable, reinstall covers and panels, and restart the operator station.
summary: This candidate captures the documented installation procedure for the Tip or A-axis cable, including routing around the gearbox and cable carrier, securing with wire ties, passing connectors through the grommet, connecting labeled M8 connectors, reinstalling the valve cover and panels, and restarting the operator station.
rough_steps: Plug the new cable into the A-axis PCA and route the cable around the gearbox and into and around the cable carrier. [Image support needed: diagram showing the A-axis PCA connection point, cable path around the gearbox, and entry into the cable carrier.]
Close all cross bars and ensure the dividers are between the cable and air line. [Image support needed: diagram showing cable carrier cross bars and divider placement between the cable and air line.]

Align the cable markings to the cable carrier and wire tie the cable in place as before, including both ends of the cable carrier and the tie point on the A-axis carriage. [Image support needed: diagram showing cable marking alignment and wire tie locations at both cable carrier ends and the A-axis carriage tie point.]
Near the A-axis PCA, verify that no wires are dangling and use wire ties as needed to contain the cable.

Route all four M8 connectors through the grommet one at a time, then push the end of the jacketed cable through the grommet. [Image support needed: diagram showing the grommet entry path and the four M8 connectors passing through.]
Connect the 3-pin M8 connector labeled "Valve" to the valve.
Connect the 4-pin M8 connector to the tote detect sensor.
Connect the 3-pin M8 connector labeled "LONG" to the longer gripper sensor.
Connect the 3-pin M8 connector labeled "SHORT" to the shorter gripper sensor.

Install the valve cover using the four M4x50 socket-head screws, make sure no wires are pinched along the cover edges, and insert the grommet into the notch for the tote sensor wire. [Image support needed: diagram showing the valve cover, screw locations, and grommet placement in the tote sensor wire notch.]
Reinstall the top and/or bottom side panels, if necessary.
Re-start the operator station using the referenced startup procedure on page 66.
expected_result: The new Tip or A-axis cable is installed, routed and secured as documented, all specified connectors are attached to their components, the valve cover and panels are reinstalled without pinched wires, and the operator station is restarted.
failure_or_escalation_notes: Stop if wires cannot be routed without dangling near the A-axis PCA or if the cable cannot be secured as documented.
Stop if the dividers cannot be positioned between the cable and air line.
Stop if any connector label or destination does not match the documented connection points.
Stop if the valve cover would pinch wires or the grommet cannot be inserted into the tote sensor wire notch.
Role set to L2_support because this is a hardware installation procedure involving internal components, cable routing, connectors, covers, and panel reinstallation.

title: Replace the Flat-Flex Cable (FFC) on the Tipper
candidate_goal: Remove the existing flat-flex cable and install a replacement FFC through the A-axis PCA, cable carrier, column, and controller PCA, then return the operator station to service.
summary: This candidate captures the documented replacement procedure for the tipper flat-flex cable, including LOTO, panel access, clamp removal and reinstallation, cable routing through the carrier and column, reconnection at the controller PCA, and restart of the operator station.
rough_steps: LOTO the tipper using the referenced Operator Station Lockout/Tagout procedure.

Gather the documented replacement part, PPE, and tools: replacement flat-flex cable, safety glasses, gloves, 3/8-in. wrench or socket, 4-mm hex wrench, 6-mm hex wrench, tool to cut wire ties, and wire ties.

If necessary, remove the nine self-tapping screws with a 3/8-in. wrench or socket and remove the station side panel to access the motor. [Image support needed: Maintenance figure showing the flat-flex cable and related clamp hardware used during FFC replacement on the tipper ClearLink controller assembly.]

At the A-axis PCA, remove the four M3 socket-head screws holding the two FFC clamps, unplug the old FFC, plug in the new FFC, and reinstall the FFC clamps with the M3 socket-head screws. [Image support needed: figure or diagram showing the A-axis PCA connection area, FFC clamps, and M3 socket-head screws.]
Note how the old FFC routes into the cable carrier and take a picture before removal.

Remove the M3 socket-head screws holding the FFC clamps, cut the one wire tie at both ends of the cable carrier, and remove the FFC clamps from the FFC while noting how the FFC feeds through the clamp. [Image support needed: figure or diagram showing clamp position on the FFC and cable carrier entry area.]
Pull the old FFC through and out of the cable carrier.
Feed the new FFC through the cable carrier, paying attention to how the FFC loops as it enters the cable carrier.

Install the FFC clamp onto the FFC as it enters the cable carrier, making sure the small leg is under the FFC; adjust the clamp placement until the loop is a similar size as when removed, then mount it with the two M3 socket-head screws. [Image support needed: figure or diagram showing the clamp orientation and the small leg under the FFC.]
Use a wire tie to secure the air line to the FFC clamp as before.

Loosen the four M8 button-head screws to release the controller box; do not remove the fasteners, and lift the box off the loosened fasteners. [Image support needed: figure or diagram showing the controller box mounting fasteners.]
Unplug the FFC from the controller PCA and pull the FFC down through and out of the column. [Image support needed: figure or diagram showing the controller PCA FFC connection.]
Feed the new FFC into and down through the column until it comes out of the upper grommet at the control box. [Image support needed: figure or diagram showing the upper grommet location.]
Plug the FFC into the controller PCA at the documented FFC connection. [Image support needed: figure or diagram labeling the FFC connection on the controller PCA.]

Hang the control box on the M8 button-head screws on the stand, feed excess cable back into the stand, and make sure no wires are pinched. Tighten the four M8 button-head screws holding the controller box to the stand. [Image support needed: Maintenance figure showing the flat-flex cable and related clamp hardware used during FFC replacement on the tipper ClearLink controller assembly.]
Reinstall the top and/or bottom side panels if they were removed.
Re-start the operator station using the referenced Starting The Operator Station procedure.

title: Remove the Tip or A-Axis Cable
candidate_goal: Safely remove the existing tip or A-axis cable from the tipper, including disconnecting connectors, opening the cable carrier, cutting retaining ties, and unplugging the cable from the A-axis PCA so it can be replaced.

rough_steps: LOTO the tipper using the referenced Operator Station Lockout/Tagout procedure.

If necessary, use a 3/8-in. wrench or socket to remove the nine self-tapping screws securing the side panel, then remove the station side panel to access the motor. [Image support needed: figure or page image showing the side panel access area near the motor.]

Remove the valve cover by removing the four M4x50 socket-head screws. Check that the correct cover and fasteners are identified before removal. [Image support needed: figure or page image showing the valve cover and the four M4x50 socket-head screws.]
Disconnect all four M8 connectors. [Image support needed: page image or figure showing the four M8 connectors.]

Pull the jacketed part of the cable out of the grommet, then feed the M8 connectors through the grommet one at a time. [Image support needed: page image showing the grommet where the jacketed cable passes through.]
Open all cross bars on the outside of the cable carrier by snapping them open. [Image support needed: page image showing the cable carrier cross bars that snap open.]

title: Repair Broken Links In The Z-Axis Cable Carrier
candidate_goal: Restore the Z-Axis cable carrier by snapping apart the carrier and repairing it when only a limited number of links are broken.
summary: The source states that the Z-Axis cable carrier can be repaired if a limited number of links are broken, and that the carrier snaps together and can be snapped apart by hand or with a small standard screwdriver. The source also provides divider placement guidance that should be checked during reassembly.
rough_steps: Put on the required PPE: safety glasses and gloves.
Obtain the listed tools and parts: replacement Z-Axis cable carrier, standard screwdriver, 1.5-mm hex wrench, and 3/8-in. wrench/socket.

Inspect the Z-Axis cable carrier and identify the broken links to confirm the repair is limited to a limited number of links. [Image support needed: figure showing the Z-Axis cable carrier assembly and individual carrier links that may be broken.]
Snap apart the cable carrier by hand or with the help of a small standard screwdriver.
Repair the cable carrier by replacing the broken link sections using the snap-together carrier construction.

Check the dividers between the cable and the air line in every second link and verify their position. [Image support needed: figure showing dividers between the cable and air line and their placement within the carrier links.]
Position the dividers all the way to one side against the air line to prevent the air line from contacting the FFC.
Reassemble the cable carrier by snapping the links back together.
expected_result: The Z-Axis cable carrier is repaired, the carrier links are snapped back together, and the dividers are positioned against the air line so the air line does not contact the FFC.
failure_or_escalation_notes: Escalate if the damage is beyond a limited number of broken links because the source distinguishes repair from full replacement but does not provide a decision threshold.
Escalate if divider placement cannot be verified or if the air line may contact the FFC after reassembly.
