title: Perform Full Replacement Of The Z-Axis Cable Carrier
candidate_goal: Replace the Z-axis cable carrier while retaining the existing air line and FFC, then verify the carrier is correctly reinstalled and the operator station is restarted.
summary: This candidate captures the documented full replacement procedure for the Z-axis cable carrier, including LOTO, panel removal, marking and preserving air line and FFC positions, removing and installing the carrier, inspection, and restart.
rough_steps: LOTO the tipper using the referenced Operator Station Lockout/Tagout procedure.

If necessary, use a 3/8-in. wrench or socket to remove the nine self-tapping screws securing the side panel, then remove the station side panel to access the motor. [Image support needed: Maintenance diagram for the routing of the tip or A-axis cable during service of the tipper assembly.]
Mark the air line and FFC at both ends where they enter and exit the cable carrier using a marker or tape.

Note how the air line is tied to the cable carrier and cut the wire ties. [Image support needed: source figure or photo showing how the air line is tied to the cable carrier before removal, if available.]

At both ends of the cable carrier, remove the M3 socket-head screws holding the FFC clamps in place. Keep the clamps on the FFC if possible. [Image support needed: source figure showing FFC clamps and M3 socket-head screw locations.]

Detach the cable carrier by removing the four M3 screws at both ends of the cable carrier. [Image support needed: source figure showing end link and M3 screw locations at both ends of the cable carrier.]
Open all cross bars on the outside of the cable carrier and remove the carrier from the FFC and air line; one end of the cable carrier may need to be snapped off to fully remove it.
Open all cross bars on the new cable carrier and probably one end.
Put the new cable carrier roughly in place while positioning the internal dividers all the way to one side, against the air line.
Snap all cross bars closed, ensuring none of the dividers are pressing on the FFC, and snap the end back on if necessary.
Mount both ends using four M3 flat-head fasteners.
Attach the FFC clamps using the M3 socket-head screws at both cable carrier ends, ensuring the FFC clamps align with the marks made earlier.
Position the air line in the cable carrier using the earlier marks and wire tie it in place.

Inspect the cable carrier to ensure all cross bars are closed, the dividers are not pressing on the FFC, and all cable carrier joints are fully seated; the cable carrier should look uniform end to end.
Reinstall the top and/or bottom side panels, if necessary.
Restart the operator station using the referenced Starting The Operator Station procedure.
expected_result: The new Z-axis cable carrier is installed with the existing air line and FFC correctly positioned, all joints and cross bars secured, panels reinstalled as needed, and the operator station restarted.
failure_or_escalation_notes: Stop if LOTO cannot be completed using the referenced procedure.
Stop if the FFC clamps do not remain identifiable or cannot be realigned to the earlier marks.
Stop if dividers press on the FFC, cross bars do not close, joints are not fully seated, or the carrier does not appear uniform end to end.
This procedure includes LOTO and component replacement, so it is not support-safe for general operator execution.
access_or_tools_needed: Tooling to remove and install M3 socket-head screws
Tooling to remove and install M3 flat-head fasteners
Access to the station side panel and cable carrier ends

title: Install Timing Pulley On Gearbox Shaft After Replacement
candidate_goal: Install the timing pulley assembly onto the gearbox shaft with the documented hole alignment, shaft key alignment, pulley spacing, and set screw torque.
summary: This candidate captures the documented installation procedure for a timing pulley replacement, including taper lock bushing assembly, shaft installation, final pulley position, alternating set screw tightening, panel reinstallation, and operator station restart reference.
rough_steps: Loosely assemble the taper lock bushing into the pulley and align all three holes.

Thread set screws into the two holes opposite each other; use the holes that have threads on the pulley half and are smooth on the bushing half. [Image support needed: figure or page image showing the set screw hole locations and the pulley half versus bushing half.]
Slip the assembly onto the shaft with the keyway slot aligned with the gearbox shaft key, if present.

Position the pulley so the final location is 8 mm (0.315 in.) from the gearbox pilot face; a spacer may be placed between the gearbox pilot face and pulley to maintain the spacing, and an 8-mm hex wrench can be used for this task. [Image support needed: figure or page image showing the 8 mm spacing between the gearbox pilot face and pulley.]
Tighten both set screws in small increments, alternating between them until the set screw torque reaches 5.6 Nm (49.5 in-lb).
Reinstall the top and/or bottom side panels, if necessary.
Re-start the operator station using the referenced procedure "Starting The Operator Station" on page 66.
expected_result: The timing pulley is installed on the shaft with aligned holes and keyway, positioned 8 mm from the gearbox pilot face, set screws tightened to 5.6 Nm, panels reinstalled as needed, and the operator station restarted.
failure_or_escalation_notes: Stop if the pulley cannot be aligned with the gearbox shaft key or the documented 8 mm spacing cannot be achieved.
Stop if the set screws cannot be tightened in the documented alternating pattern to 5.6 Nm.
This source excerpt is installation-only and does not provide full safety isolation or guarded-access prerequisites; use higher-skill support review before execution.

Torque tool capable of 5.6 Nm (49.5 in-lb)
Access to top and/or bottom side panels

title: Remove Timing Pulley From Taper-Lock Bushing and Shaft
candidate_goal: Remove the timing pulley assembly from the shaft by freeing the pulley from the taper-lock bushing using the documented set screw method.
summary: This candidate captures the documented mechanical removal procedure for a timing pulley, including LOTO, side panel removal if needed, set screw removal, use of the threaded release hole, and sliding the pulley assembly off the shaft.
rough_steps: LOTO the tipper using the referenced Operator Station Lockout/Tagout procedure.

If necessary, use a 3/8-in. wrench or socket to remove the nine self-tapping screws securing the side panel, then remove the station side panel to access the motor. [Image support needed: Maintenance diagram for the timing pulley assembly used during timing pulley replacement.]
Use a 1/8-in. hex wrench to remove both set screws from the bushing. Do not use the round end of the hex wrench.

Identify the hole between the two set screw holes that has threads on the taper-lock bushing side and is smooth on the pulley side. [Image support needed: diagram showing the set screw locations, taper-lock bushing, and pulley relationship.]
Thread one removed set screw into that center hole.
Tighten the set screw to break the pulley free from the taper-lock bushing. The source notes this may take some work to get the pulley free.
If needed, push or pull the pulley by hand, but do not hit the pulley with a hammer because the source states this will damage the gearbox bearings.
Once the pulley is free, slide everything off the shaft.
expected_result: The timing pulley is freed from the taper-lock bushing and the pulley assembly slides off the shaft.
failure_or_escalation_notes: Do not strike the pulley with a hammer; the source states this will damage the bearings in the gearbox.
Escalate if the pulley does not break free using the documented set screw method, because no additional removal method is provided in the source.
This procedure involves LOTO and mechanical disassembly, so it is not operator-safe.

title: Remove the Idler Pulley Assembly
candidate_goal: Safely remove the idler pulley assembly from the tipper column so it can be replaced.
summary: This candidate captures the documented removal procedure for the idler pulley assembly, including lockout/tagout, side panel removal for motor access, securing the A-axis and counterbalance before releasing belt tension, removing belt clamps, loosening tensioning screws, and sliding the assembly out of the column.
rough_steps: LOTO the tipper using the documented Operator Station Lockout/Tagout procedure referenced by the manual.

If needed to access the motor, use a 3/8-in. wrench or socket to remove the nine self-tapping fasteners securing the station side panel, then remove the side panel. [Image support needed: figure or photo showing the station side panel and motor access area.]

Manually position the A-axis and the counterbalance between the threaded holes in the actuator base. There are two holes adjacent to each rail, and the carriages should be positioned between the holes on the respective rails.

Thread M6 socket-head screws into the holes below both carriages to prevent the A-axis and counterbalance from dropping once belt tension is released. [Image support needed: figure or photo showing the actuator base holes, rails, carriage positions, and screw locations below both carriages.]

Remove the belt clamps by removing the six M6x20 countersunk screws from each clamp with a 5-mm hex wrench to free the carriages from the belt. [Image support needed: figure or photo identifying the belt clamps and M6x20 screw locations.]
Release belt tension by loosening the two M6x35 socket-head screws on the bottom side of the assembly. [Image support needed: figure or photo showing the bottom-side M6 tensioning screw locations.]

Remove the two M8x25 socket-head screws holding the idler pulley assembly in place, then slide the idler pulley assembly out of the column. [Image support needed: figure or photo showing the idler pulley assembly mounting screws and removal direction.]
If needed for replacement, transfer the M6x35 tensioning screws from the removed idler pulley assembly to the new assembly.
expected_result: The idler pulley assembly is removed from the column, with the A-axis and counterbalance secured against dropping and the tensioning screws available for transfer if needed.
failure_or_escalation_notes: This procedure includes LOTO, manual positioning of mechanical components, belt tension release, and component removal, so it should be handled by L2 support rather than an operator.
Stop if the A-axis or counterbalance cannot be safely positioned and secured below both carriages before belt tension is released.
Stop if the documented fasteners, holes, clamps, or screw locations cannot be positively identified from the source-backed equipment layout.
access_or_tools_needed: M6 socket-head screws for securing below both carriages
Access to the station side panel, motor area, actuator base, rails, belt clamps, and idler pulley assembly

source_refs.quote_or_summary: Figure 7-21 appears on page 185 in section optisweep_operation_and_maintenance_manual_final_1_7_3_20_1_removal.

extraction_notes: Procedure type set to recovery because the source documents a component-removal sequence used to restore the system through replacement work.
Role set to L2_support because the procedure includes LOTO, guarded/mechanical access, manual positioning of moving components, belt tension release, and hardware removal.
The source section is removal-only; installation or retensioning details are not included here and were not invented.
Figure support is referenced, but only source-backed visual intents were included.

title: Replace The Tipper Assembly
candidate_goal: Remove an existing tipper and install a replacement tipper on the existing sub-plate using the documented service disconnection and reconnection steps.
summary: This candidate covers the documented tipper replacement procedure, including lockout/tagout, optional side-panel removal for access, disconnecting power, Ethernet, air, and conduit services, removing the supported tipper from the sub-plate, installing the replacement tipper, reconnecting services, and restarting the operator station.
rough_steps: Apply lockout/tagout to the tipper as referenced by the manual's Operator Station Lockout/Tagout procedure.
Put on the required PPE: safety glasses and gloves.

If access is needed, use a 3/8-in. wrench or socket to remove the nine self-tapping fasteners securing the side panel, then remove the station side panel to access the motor. [Image support needed: Maintenance figure showing the flat-flex cable and related clamp hardware used during FFC replacement on the tipper ClearLink controller assembly.]
Remove the control box and disconnect the power cable at the 6-pin Molex connector, then push the cable into the column.
Unplug the Ethernet cable from the ClearLink controller and push it into the column.
Disconnect the air line at the quick disconnect at the bottom of the column, leaving the quick disconnect with the tipper side of the air line.
Add tape over the air line and quick disconnect to prevent debris from entering either side of the air line.
Fully disconnect the conduit bringing power, air, and Ethernet to the column, and pull these cables free from the tipper.
Optionally remove the gripper and the counter-balance weights using the referenced procedures if needed for safer or easier replacement.
Support the tipper and remove the four hex head screws holding the tipper to the sub-plate.
Install the new tipper onto the existing sub-plate.
Reconnect the conduit, power, air, and Ethernet to the column and control box.
Mount the gripper again if it was removed.
Reinstall the top and/or bottom side panels if they were removed.
Restart the operator station using the referenced startup procedure.
expected_result: The replacement tipper is mounted to the existing sub-plate, required power, Ethernet, air, and conduit connections are restored, removed panels or related components are reinstalled as needed, and the operator station is restarted.
failure_or_escalation_notes: Stop if lockout/tagout cannot be applied using the referenced procedure.
Stop if the tipper cannot be safely supported during removal.
Escalate if service connections, conduit, or mounting hardware cannot be disconnected or reconnected as documented.
Escalate if the operator station cannot be restarted after installation.
access_or_tools_needed: 3/8-in. wrench or socket if side-panel removal is necessary
Access to the station side panel, control box, ClearLink controller, air-line quick disconnect, conduit, and sub-plate mounting screws
Referenced Starting The Operator Station procedure
Referenced Gripper Replacement procedure if gripper removal is performed
Referenced Counter-balance weights procedure if weight removal is performed

title: Set Up a Replacement Scanner Using Barcode Interface
candidate_goal: Configure a replacement scanner so it is powered, connected, calibrated if needed, and updated with the documented scanner settings.
summary: This candidate covers the documented end-to-end setup of a replacement scanner using a Windows laptop, Zebra DS3608-SR dock hardware, Barcode Interface.exe, connectivity verification, settings access, prefix verification, and HTTP/OPC URL updates.
rough_steps: Gather the documented requirements: a Windows 10 or 11 laptop with Barcode Interface.exe installed, a Zebra DS3608-SR scanner dock, USB cable, power adapter, two 120 VAC outlets, and network connectivity to the AGV and Hospital control validated by pinging their IP addresses. [Image support needed: Artifact from the Scanner Setup section showing or accompanying the procedure for configuring a replacement Zebra DS3608-SR scanner.]

Connect the RJ45 side of the USB cable to the back of the scanner dock, connect the USB side to the laptop, connect the auxiliary lead to the power adapter, and plug the adapter into a 120 VAC outlet. Confirm the dock lights up. [Image support needed: scanner dock connections for RJ45, USB, auxiliary power, and dock light status.]

If the scanner is uncharged, place it on the dock and wait until the red battery symbol shown on top turns green. [Image support needed: scanner battery symbol changing from red to green while resting on the dock.]
Log into the laptop with a FORTNA account.
Launch the Barcode Interface application from the desktop. [Image support needed: Barcode Interface.exe on the desktop with a FORTNA logo.]

Check the connectivity indicator at the bottom right of the screen. If it is red, click "Calibrate Scanner" and follow the onscreen instructions; if it is green, continue. [Image support needed: Barcode Interface screen showing the bottom-right connectivity indicator and the "Calibrate Scanner" control.]
Open scanner settings by clicking the settings button and supplying the password. By default, the password is "Opti." [Image support needed: settings button and password entry for scanner settings.]
Verify the configured prefixes match the labels on the totes and locations. [Image support needed: settings screen area showing prefix values to compare against tote and location labels.]

Change the URL for the HTTP setting to contain the AGV control IP address and change the URL for the OPC setting to contain the Hospital control IP address respectively. [Image support needed: settings screen showing HTTP URL and OPC URL fields.]
Save the configuration with the "Save Settings" button. [Image support needed: settings screen showing the "Save Settings" button.]
expected_result: The replacement scanner is connected and powered, connectivity is acceptable or calibrated per the application, prefixes match tote and location labels, HTTP and OPC URL settings contain the documented control IP addresses, and the settings are saved.
failure_or_escalation_notes: Stop if the dock does not light up after the documented power and cable connections are made.
Stop if the scanner does not charge to a green battery indication when placed on the dock.
Escalate if network connectivity to the AGV and Hospital control cannot be validated by ping.
Escalate if settings access is blocked and the documented password does not work.
Escalate if the required AGV control or Hospital control IP addresses are not available.
access_or_tools_needed: Barcode Interface.exe installed on the laptop
FORTNA account login for the laptop
Network connectivity to AGV and Hospital control

title: Inspect and lubricate tipper Z-axis linear bearings
candidate_goal: Visually inspect the tipper Z-axis linear rails for grease condition and lubricate the bearing blocks when the rails are dry.
summary: Source describes a preventive maintenance task for both Z-axis linear rails, including the visual condition to check, the grease type, the number and location of grease nipples, and the grease quantity per block.
rough_steps: Visually inspect both Z-axis linear rails for wetness of grease and/or signs of dried grease.
If the rails are dry (not wet with grease), lubricate the bearing blocks using automotive bearing grease (for example, Mobil1™).
Apply grease at the bearing block nipples; there are two bearing blocks, with one standard 1/4-in. nipple on each end of each block, for a total of four nipples.
Add 1.0 to 3.0 cc of grease to each block.
Do not treat wiping grease on the rails as adequate lubrication.
expected_result: Both Z-axis linear rails have been inspected for grease condition, and if the rails were dry, each bearing block has been lubricated with the specified grease quantity through the grease nipples.
failure_or_escalation_notes: The source does not provide escalation criteria beyond the lubrication instruction and note that wiping grease on the rails is not adequate.
access_or_tools_needed: Means to apply 1.0 to 3.0 cc of grease to each bearing block
Access to both Z-axis linear rails and bearing block grease nipples

source_refs.quote_or_summary: Visually inspect both Z-axis linear rails for wetness of grease and/or signs of dried grease. If dry, lubricate bearing blocks using automotive bearing grease. Wiping grease on the rails is not adequate.

extraction_notes: This content appears on the same page after the daily checklist under section 7.2.1 and is included because it contains explicit procedural steps in the provided section_text.
Role set to L2_support because this is preventive maintenance involving component lubrication and physical access to machine bearing blocks.
No additional safety controls or access prerequisites were added because they are not stated in the source.

evidence_source_refs.quote_or_summary: Visually inspect both Z-axis linear rails for wetness of grease and/or signs of dried grease. If dry, lubricate bearing blocks using automotive bearing grease. Wiping grease on the rails is not adequate.

title: Remove a limit or home sensor
candidate_goal: Safely remove a limit or home sensor from the tipper, including disconnecting it from the correct connection point and freeing the routed cable.
summary: This section provides the removal portion of replacing a limit or home sensor. It identifies required PPE and tools, distinguishes A-axis and Z-axis sensor cable/connection differences, and gives source-backed removal steps including LOTO, unplugging the sensor, removing the retaining screw, and pulling the sensor/cable free.
rough_steps: Gather the replacement limit sensor or home sensor, safety glasses, gloves, a 1.5-mm hex wrench, a tool to cut wire ties, and wire ties.

Identify the sensor type and axis details before removal: home sensors are normally open (NO), limit sensors are normally closed (NC), Z-axis sensors have long cables (>6 ft), and A-axis sensors have shorter cables (<2 ft).
LOTO the tipper as referenced by the manual's "Operator Station Lockout/Tagout" procedure.
Unplug the sensor from its connection point: A-axis sensors plug into a printed circuit assembly (PCA) near the A-axis motor, and Z-axis sensors plug into the CLINK controller inside the control box.

Remove the M3 button-head screw holding the sensor in place. [Image support needed: Maintenance figure showing the flat-flex cable and related clamp hardware used during FFC replacement on the tipper ClearLink controller assembly.]
Feed the sensor through the hole used for the wire routing as it is removed.
For a Z-axis sensor, pull the sensor from the column.
For an A-axis sensor, pull the defective sensor away; cut the wire tie if needed to free the cable.
If helpful during replacement, connect the new sensor to the one being replaced before pulling through the cable routing so the new cable follows the same path.
expected_result: The limit or home sensor is disconnected, unfastened, and removed from its mounting/routing path, with the cable freed from the machine for replacement.
failure_or_escalation_notes: Use LOTO before performing the removal.
If the sensor cable cannot be freed because of routing or wire ties beyond what is described, the source does not provide additional recovery detail.
Sensor location details are referenced on the previous page and are not included in this section.
access_or_tools_needed: Replacement part: limit sensor or home sensor
Access to PCA near the A-axis motor for A-axis sensors
Access to the CLINK controller inside the control box for Z-axis sensors
extraction_notes: This section contains only the removal subsection; installation steps are not present in the provided source.
Role set to L2_support because the procedure requires LOTO, component removal, and access inside the control box.
The note about connecting the new sensor to the old one is included as source-backed handling guidance, even though full installation is not provided.
No related artifacts or operational context IDs were provided in the input.

title: Back up Teknic SD motor parameters before replacement
candidate_goal: Preserve the custom parameters from a Teknic SD motor by connecting to the motor and using the manufacturer application before motor or gearbox replacement work.
summary: This section provides the required tools, safety caution, and software download/setup details needed to connect to a Teknic SD motor and save its custom parameters prior to replacement. It notes that both Z-axis and A-axis motors are mounted into gearboxes which may also be replaced.
rough_steps: Use caution when lifting because components may be heavy.

Gather the listed items: replacement Teknic SD motor, safety glasses, gloves, torque wrench, 4-mm hex wrench, 5-mm hex wrench, 3/8-in. wrench/socket, laptop, USB-A to micro-USB cable, 25-V power supply, 75-V power supply, 4-pin Molex power supply connector, and 8-pin Molex communication cable.

Identify the Teknic SD motor to be worked on; the source notes the Z-axis and A-axis motors are mounted into gearboxes which may also be replaced. [Image support needed: figure showing the Teknic SD motor and how it is distinguished from the gearbox assembly.]
Connect a cable to the motor so the motor can be accessed by the manufacturer application.
Download the manufacturer application from https://teknic.com/downloads/.
In the download selections, choose "ClearPath," "SD" for the motor type, and "NEMA 23/34 IP53 Motor Body."
Run the manufacturer application to save the motor's custom parameters.
expected_result: The Teknic manufacturer application is obtained with the specified selections and the motor's custom parameters are saved before replacement work proceeds.
failure_or_escalation_notes: If the motor cannot be connected or its custom parameters cannot be saved using the manufacturer application, stop and escalate because the source indicates the parameters should be saved before replacement.
Use caution with heavy components to avoid injury or equipment damage.

title: Download motor configuration from a matching motor and upload it to the replacement motor
candidate_goal: Preserve and transfer the correct motor configuration for a replacement motor by saving the configuration from the motor being replaced or from a working motor in the same position, then uploading that configuration to the new motor.

From the File menu, select Save Configuration and save the configuration as an .mtr file.
Disconnect from the motor after saving the configuration file.
Upload the saved configuration file to the new motor.
If the new motor will be tested, make sure it is clamped down before testing.

title: Remove the pneumatic valve cover and disconnect the valve electrical connector
candidate_goal: Gain access to the pneumatic valve and begin valve removal by safely opening the valve area and disconnecting the M8 electrical connector.
summary: This section provides the initial pneumatic valve replacement/removal actions: apply LOTO to the tipper, remove the station side panel if needed for access, remove the valve cover fasteners, and disconnect the M8 electrical connector from the valve.
rough_steps: Gather the required replacement part, PPE, and tools: pneumatic valve, safety glasses, gloves, 2.5-mm hex wrench, 3-mm hex wrench, 5-mm hex wrench, 6-mm hex wrench, 3/8-in. wrench or socket, and Loctite.
LOTO the tipper as referenced by "Operator Station Lockout/Tagout" on page 2.

If necessary, use a 3/8-in. wrench or socket to remove the nine self-tapping fasteners securing the side panel, then remove the station side panel to access the motor. [Image support needed: station side panel access area and surrounding valve area.]
Remove the four M4 socket-head screws holding the valve cover in place. [Image support needed: valve cover and the four M4 socket-head screws identified on the pneumatic valve assembly.]

title: Repair or replace the Z-Axis cable carrier
candidate_goal: Restore the Z-Axis cable carrier by replacing the full carrier or repairing it when only a limited number of links are broken, while maintaining proper divider placement between the cable and air line.
summary: The source describes a maintenance procedure to repair or fully replace the Z-Axis cable carrier. The carrier snaps together and can be snapped apart by hand or with a small standard screwdriver. The section also specifies required PPE, tools, replacement part, and the required divider position to keep the air line from contacting the FFC.
rough_steps: Put on the required PPE: safety glasses and gloves.
Gather the required items: replacement Z-Axis cable carrier, standard screwdriver, 1.5-mm hex wrench, and 3/8-in. wrench/socket.
Inspect the Z-Axis cable carrier to determine whether the full carrier needs replacement or whether repair is possible because only a limited number of links are broken.
Snap the cable carrier apart by hand or use a small standard screwdriver to help separate the links as needed.
Replace the full Z-Axis cable carrier or repair the carrier by addressing the broken links, using the replacement part as needed.
Check the dividers between the cable and the air line in every second link. It is acceptable if several dividers are missing.
Position the dividers all the way to one side against the air line to prevent the air line from contacting the FFC.

Observe the cable carrier assembly and divider locations during the work. [Image support needed: figure showing the Z-Axis cable carrier assembly, example carrier links, and divider position between the cable and air line.]
expected_result: The Z-Axis cable carrier is repaired or replaced, the carrier links are reassembled, and the dividers are positioned against the air line so the air line does not contact the FFC.
failure_or_escalation_notes: Stop if the source does not provide enough detail to safely continue disassembly or reassembly beyond snapping links apart.
Escalate if the cable carrier damage is beyond a limited number of broken links or if proper divider positioning cannot be restored.

title: Quick replacement of the Z-axis cable carrier
candidate_goal: Replace the Z-axis cable carrier by reusing the existing mounted end pieces when both ends of the carrier are in good condition.
summary: Source-backed quick replacement procedure for the Z-axis cable carrier. The procedure uses lockout/tagout, side panel removal if needed, disconnects the carrier from both mounted end pieces, transfers in a new carrier, positions internal dividers against the air line, closes the cross bars, and snaps the new carrier into the existing end pieces.
rough_steps: Lock out/tag out the tipper as referenced by the source.

If needed, use a 3/8-in. wrench or socket to remove the nine self-tapping fasteners securing the side panel, then remove the station side panel to access the motor. [Image support needed: Maintenance figure identifying the pneumatic cylinder referenced in the pneumatic cylinder replacement procedure.]

Snap apart the cable carrier at both ends, leaving the end pieces attached to the tipper. [Image support needed: figure or photo showing the cable carrier end pieces that remain mounted to the tipper.]
Snap the ends off the new cable carrier and open all cross bars on the outside of the new cable carrier.
Open all cross bars on the outside of the old cable carrier and remove the old cable carrier from the system.
Place the new cable carrier roughly in position and move the internal dividers all the way to one side, against the air line.
Snap all cross bars closed.
Snap both ends of the new cable carrier into the end pieces that remain mounted.
expected_result: The new cable carrier is installed in place of the old carrier, the cross bars are closed, the internal dividers are positioned against the air line, and both ends are snapped into the existing mounted end pieces.
failure_or_escalation_notes: This quick replacement is only stated to apply when both ends of the carrier are in good condition.

title: Load tipper firmware from a USB drive using HMI boot mode
candidate_goal: Install a new firmware version on the tipper controller using the HMI and confirm the controller returns to the expected screens after reboot.
summary: This procedure powers down the tipper, enters a gray-screen firmware load mode by holding the top three HMI buttons during power-up, installs firmware from a USB drive, then reboots the controller and verifies the HMI returns to the Visu_Default and Visu_MCP_Dual screens.
rough_steps: Turn off power to the tipper with the disconnect.
Press and hold the top three buttons on the HMI. [Image support needed: HMI screenshot related to controller setup and network verification during tipper commissioning.]
Continue holding the top three HMI buttons while powering on the tipper. [Image support needed: HMI screenshot related to controller setup and network verification during tipper commissioning.]
Keep holding the buttons until a gray screen appears on the HMI. [Image support needed: HMI screenshot related to controller setup and network verification during tipper commissioning.]

Plug the USB drive containing the new firmware version into the USB dongle on the HMI. [Image support needed: HMI screenshot related to controller setup and network verification during tipper commissioning.]
Press the INSTALL FROM FILE button. [Image support needed: HMI screenshot related to controller setup and network verification during tipper commissioning.]
Load the firmware from the USB drive.
Power the controller off and back on, and allow it to boot normally without pressing any buttons.

Verify that the HMI shows the "Visu_Default" screen displaying the IP and subnet mask settings. [Image support needed: HMI screenshot related to controller setup and network verification during tipper commissioning.]
Press F3 to go to the "Visu_MCP_Dual" screen. [Image support needed: HMI screenshot related to controller setup and network verification during tipper commissioning.]
expected_result: The firmware is loaded from the USB drive, the controller reboots normally, the HMI displays the Visu_Default screen with IP and subnet mask settings, and F3 opens the Visu_MCP_Dual screen.
failure_or_escalation_notes: If the gray screen does not appear while holding the top three HMI buttons during power-up, stop and escalate.
If the firmware cannot be loaded from the USB drive, stop and escalate.
If the controller does not boot normally after the power cycle, stop and escalate.
If the Visu_Default screen does not appear or does not display the IP and subnet mask settings, stop and escalate.
If F3 does not open the Visu_MCP_Dual screen, stop and escalate.
access_or_tools_needed: USB drive containing the new firmware version
